affected_groups: Roma and low-income families relying on informal escort arrangements
Regional bus operators refusing unaccompanied minors
The centralized hub school (risk of re-segregation via exit)
content.decision_question: Equity and child safety versus system efficiency: the scenario's administrative/pedagogical rationale for centralization is weighed against the physical safety and equal treatment of the youngest, most vulnerable commuters
content.evidence_boundary: Partly settled, partly contested: it is documented that regional buses refuse unaccompanied minors and that informal parent-escort shifts currently fill this gap — this is a factual baseline. What remains contested/unknown is the actual scale of the safety risk increase under S2's specific routing, whether Roma/low-income households are disproportionately affected (no disaggregated data cited), and whether exit/white-flight dynamics would materialize at the level feared; these require site-specific transport audits and monitoring data not yet available
content.tension: Centralizing upper grades to a distant hub shifts daily commuting burden and safety risk onto 10-14-year-olds — especially Roma and low-income children already relying on informal parent escort shifts because regional buses refuse unaccompanied minors — with no binding, funded transport-safety mechanism built into the design.
content.title: Decision tension A5: S2
value_poles: system efficiency: the scenario's administrative/pedagogical rationale for centralization is weighed against the physical safety and equal treatment of the youngest, most vulnerable commuters
